1. selection of a fuel system which meets nuclear and thermal requirements;
Solutions of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ in certain mixed fluorides such as NaF - BeF $_2$ , NaF - ZrF $_4$ , Li $^{7}$ F - BeF $_2$ , or DF - NaF.
1. extraction between a liquid-metal phase, of which bismuth, iron, lead, nickel, aluminum, or uranium is the principal constituent, and a fused-salt phase consisting of mixed fluorides or chlorides;
2. extraction between a uranium-rich liquid-metal phase and molten silver or copper;
3. fractional distillation from a liquid fuel containing volatile constituents, notably the distillation of $\mathrm{UCl}_4$ from a chloride fuel or $\mathrm{UF}_6$ from a fluoride fuel;
5. selective precipitation of intermetallic compounds from a uranium-rich liquid phase;
6. fractional crystallization of one or more constituents from a liquid fuel.

The most valuable research program will have the dual objectives of (1) obtaining detailed information on the chemical properties of the substances listed above, and (2) making fundamental advances in high-temperature inorganic and physical chemistry.

Since suggestions for developmental research related to corrosion and to engineering are given in the report, "Engineering analysis of Non-Aqueous Fluid-Fuel Reactors" (MIT-5002), the proposals for developmental research in this report are limited to separation processes.

As a very rough guide for setting a limit to the allowable amounts of moderating, low mass-number diluents, Brooks (1) suggested that the ratio of the number of atoms of diluent to the number of atoms of fuel (U-235) should be less than half the atomic weight of the diluent.
This temperature may be of the order of $700^{\circ} - 800^{\circ}$ C.
Tabulations of low-melting binary eutectics have been reported by Grimes and Hill (2) and by Shaw and Boulger (3).
The melting points are $835^{\circ}$ C. and $590^{\circ}$ C., respectively.
Information on binary mixtures of uranium trichloride or tetrachloride and other salts is contained almost entirely in one report of work conducted at Brown University (4) in 1943.

Data for fluoride fuel mixtures of interest for thermal reactors, such as the ARE (5), (6), (7), have been helpful in the prediction of physical properties, e.g., viscosities, of analogous chloride mixtures.

The systems UCl₃-KCl and UCl₄-KCl were considered inferior to the corresponding binary systems containing NaCl because of the formation of compounds of the type 2KCl-UCl₃ and 2KCl-UCl₄ having congruent melting points at $625^{\circ}$ C. and $650^{\circ}$ C., respectively.
In contrast, the phase diagram for the system NaCl-UCl₃ consists of a single eutectic at 33 mole per cent UCl₃ with a melting point of $520^{\circ}$ C. as shown in Figure II-1.
For the system NaCl-UCl₄ the compound 2NaCl-UCl₄ having an incongruent melting point at $430^{\circ}$ C. is formed.
The phase diagram showing the eutectic at 50 mole per cent UCl₄ and a temperature of $370^{\circ}$ C. is represented in Figure II-2.
The phase diagram for the NaCl-PbCl $_2$ system is shown in Figure II-3.
The data are those of Treis (8).

The two predicted ternary eutectic melting points were: $320^\circ \mathrm{C}$ . for System A having the composition $\mathsf{PbCl}_2$ , 0.33 moles; NaCl, 0.33 moles; UCl $_4$ , 0.33 moles; and $250^\circ \mathrm{C}$ . for System B with the composition $\mathsf{PbCl}_2$ , 0.43 moles; NaCl, 0.17 moles; UCl $_4$ , 0.40 moles.
Kaufmann (9), there are three breaks in the cooling curve for System A at about $524^\circ \mathrm{C}$ , $356^\circ \mathrm{C}$ , and $290^\circ \mathrm{C}$ .
For System B there are two breaks; one at $315^\circ \mathrm{C}$ and the other at $295^\circ \mathrm{C}$ .

Kraus (4) found a nearly linear liquidus curve up to 20 mole per cent UCl₃, the same liquidus temperature, $565^{\circ}$ C., for 30 per cent as for 20 per cent, and no evidence of a eutectic halt in this range.
Butler (10), in the Ames work discussed on page 488 of Katz and Rabino-witch (11), agrees with this.
Kraus also carried one 20 per cent solution to high temperatures and found a break in the cooling curve at $830^{\circ}$ C.

He says, therefore, "The two compounds are only slightly soluble in each other in the liquid phase." Calkins (12) found breaks at $838^{\circ}$ C. and $545^{\circ}$ C. for 89% UCl₃, and says, "The UCl₃-UCl₄ phase diagram might be quite similar to the UBr₃-UBr₄ diagram." During operation of the reactor, the isotopic composition of the uranium is altered by the formation of U-236 and U-237.
Too much U-236 is harmful because it dilutes the fuel and is a source of undesirable Pu-238.
The U-236 is maintained at an acceptable level by isotopic processing in a K-25 type separation plant.
The U-237 is a short-lived (6.63d) $\beta^{-}\gamma$ emitter and introduces a holdup problem in the decontamination of uranium to be sent to the isotope separation plant.
For each fission product the properties of the chemical species which are likely to be present in the fused-salt mixture at a temperature of $700^{\circ} - 800^{\circ}$ C. are given in Table II-3.
The selection of likely species was based on thermodynamic considerations, and on descriptive information contained in Quill (14), Sidgwick (17), Latimer (18), and Hückel (19).

The values for the melting points, boiling points, heats of formation, and free energies of formation given in Table II-3 were obtained from Quill (14), to a limited extent from Latimer (18), and from NBS circular 500 (20).
The significance of parentheses and brackets around numbers in the table is the same as for Table II-1.
As in Table II-1, the vapor pressures are for the pure substances as liquids at $1000^{\circ}$ K. and were obtained for supercooled liquids in some cases.
For a few of the chlorides the vapor pressures at $1000^{\circ}$ K. were estimated from values at $500^{\circ}$ K.
These chlorides, together with their vapor pressures at $500^{\circ}$ K., are given in Table II-4.

For some of the substances the critical temperature is below $1000^{\circ}$ K.
However, the product of the extrapolated vapor pressure given in Table II-3 and the activity is a convenient approximate measure of the volatility in dilute solutions.
The contribution of each fission product, as the element, to the total of fission products is given both as an atom per cent and as a weight per cent in Table II-3.

The assumptions used in estimating the atom per cent values were (a) that the thermal fission yields for U-235 may be taken as a reasonable approximation to the fast fission yields, and (b) that the reactor had operated for several months without removal of fission products.
Fission chain yields were obtained from the Plutonium Project (21) and from Coryell and Sugarman (22).
Such fission products with half-lives greater than 100 days are listed in Table II-5.
For the weight per cent values in Table II-3, an average mass number was assigned to each fission element.
For example, $82\%$ by wieght of the fission products results from the eleven elements: Sr, Zr, Mo, Ru, Xe, Cs, Ba, La, Ce, Pr, and Nd.

If, for example, a uniform processing rate of $2\%$ of the fuel mixture per day with complete recovery is assumed, the mean processing age or residence time of the fission products becomes fifty days.
For a fission product production rate of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ , per day, the fuel would contain $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ . of fission products at steady state.
For example, the yield of chain-95 is distributed along the chain approximately as follows:

\begin{array}{c} \mathrm {Zr} ^ {95} \\ 65 \% \end{array} \xrightarrow {\quad 65 \mathrm {d} \quad} \begin{array}{c} \mathrm {Nb} ^ {95} \\ 18 \% \end{array} \xrightarrow {\quad 35 \mathrm {d} \quad} \begin{array}{c} \mathrm {Mo} ^ {95} \\ 17 \% \end{array} \quad (\mathrm {s}).
Similarly, the yields of other chains such as those of mass 89, 91, and 103 will be distributed along the chains, and the total yields for each fission element given in Table II-3 will be altered.
This is based on the yields in Table II-3.
In the case of ruthenium, for example, the trichloride shows appreciable decomposition to the elements at temperatures around $700^{\circ}$ C.

Grimes and Hill (2), in a literature survey of high-temperature fuel systems, refer to experiments conducted at Westinghouse on the electrodeposition of uranium from a molten bath containing $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ , $\mathbf{KF}$ , $\mathbf{CaCl}_{2}$ , and $\mathbf{NaCl}$ at $800^{\circ}$ to $900^{\circ}$ C.

Reference is made in the Liquid Metals Handbook (25) to the use of Types 304 and 316 stainless steels up to about $900^{\circ}$ C. for the distillation of metallic potassium from a potassium chloride-sodium melt.
For example, Inconel appears to show some promise as a container material for the fused-fluoride mixtures being considered by the ANP Group at Oak Ridge (7).
At Y-12 (24) it was found that silver is the most corrosion-resistant of the practical metals for the handling of uranium hexafluoride.
Illium R has been recommended for UF reactors at the Mallinckrodt Chemical Works (26).
Strictly on the basis of the free energies of formation of chlorides at $1000^{\circ}$ K., estimated from data given by Quill (14), the elements in Table II-6 should be corrosion resistant.
The estimated free energy changes at $1000^{\circ}$ K. for the reaction between the element and UCl₄ to give UCl₃ and the chloride listed has been included in Table II-6.
With respect to uranium halides, the free energies for reaction of container metals with $\mathrm{UCl}_4$ and $\mathrm{UF}_4$ differ appreciably as shown in Table II-7.
From the data for the constituents of Inconel in Table II-7, it can be predicted, in a qualitative way, that all three metals will react with UCl $_4$ and UF $_4$ .
Even for a value of $\Delta F^0$ as high as +25, the extent of reaction may be sufficient to permit thermal mass transport as pointed out by Bredig (7).

The enthalpy changes for these reactions, also listed in Table II-7, indicate that the equilibrium constants decrease with increasing temperature for corrosion of chromium by either UCl $_4$ or UF $_4$ and of iron by UCl $_4$ , which would lead to mass transfer from cold to hot container walls.

The recent work by Bareis (1) at BNL has pointed toward a potentially simple and powerful method for the treatment of nuclear reactor fuels in order to remove and possibly to separate the products of reaction.

Bareis' experiments have been concerned with the processing of liquid uranium-bismuth alloys by means of an equilibration of such liquids with a fused-salt phase (consisting of the eutectic mixture: 59 mole % LiCl-41 mole % KCl).
Grimes (2) of ORNL suggests the processing of fused fluorides by equilibration with liquid zinc.)
The sealed capsule then was placed in a constant temperature bath and allowed to equilibrate overnight at a temperature near $500^{\circ}$ C.
In later work (5) the procedure was varied slightly by first filtering the salt phase and then sealing below a coarse pyrex filter, while the bismuth alloy was placed above the filter.
After sealing, equilibration took place at about $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
In Figure III-1, which is taken from Bareis (1), is presented a summary of the distribution of the elements Ce, La, and Pr between U-Bi alloys and a fused eutectic melt of KCl-LiCl.
In Table III-1, also taken from Bareis (4, 5, 6, 7) are the data pertinent to the experiments summarized in Figure III-1.

A line of unit slope may be fitted to the lower curve of Figure III-1, which emphasizes this result.
Indeed, the slope of the upper line in Figure III-1 suggests that the concentration in the metal phase is roughly proportional to the third or fourth power of the concentration in the salt phase.

Inasmuch as the uranium content of the systems examined was limited in extent, it seems reasonable to conclude from Figure III-1, that for increasing concentrations of the materials mentioned and for fixed uranium concentrations there will be increasing departure from a constant "distribution-coefficient."

(6) to estimate a value of $\underline{n}$ .
The data do not permit unequivocal determination of its value, but a value between 1.5 and 2 is in reasonable accord with the data.

In view of the uncertainties involved, the value $n = 2$ is adopted here for convenience and implies that the elements lanthanum, cerium, and praseodymium, insofar as they can be grouped together as regards these experiments, exist in the salt phase as sub-halides rather than normal halides.
There is some evidence for this for certain of the rare-earths (8), which are presumably similar to the elements treated here.

If three per cent of the Pu is left in the metal phase in one stage, for example, Figure III-7 indicates that the $\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]$ should be increased to $10^{-25}$ atm. and one per cent of the U would also be extracted.

Processing requirements for reactors with high burn-up rates for the fuel, such as the fast fused-chloride reactors, are complicated by the formation of higher isotopes, U-236 and U-237.

capable of meeting these requirements but lead to complex and expensive procedures which do not satisfactorily meet requirements 6 to 9 of section 2.3 Accordingly, at this time the most promising way of utilizing separation processes carried out directly on liquid metals or fused salts is for preseparation or rough concentration of plutonium and fission products into one or more crude products.
Figure IV-2 is representative of flowsheets based on the use of rough preseparation steps.
This possibility is realized in a flowsheet like that in Figure IV-2 in which each step returns unfinished intermediate products to a preceding step, or eventually back to the reactor.

As already discussed in section 2 of this chapter, the proper role of these wet processes in liquid-fuel systems is in the steps of purifying or decontaminating the final products leaving the system, especially the plutonium product.
A convenient basis of classifying liquid-metal unit processes is according to whether the second phase into which the elements are separated is (1) gas, (2) liquid, or (3) solid.
The common techniques of removing gases include (1) simple heating to a high temperature at atmospheric pressure, (2) flushing with an inert gas, and (3) vacuum degassing.
Preliminary tests of the separation of gaseous fission products from melted uranium fuel elements gave promising results in the Ames Laboratory (2).
Brewer has considered this process on thermodynamic grounds (3).
Experiments reported from Chalk River (4) indicate that possible volatilization and loss of plutonium must be considered.
The extracting phase may be (1) another liquid metal, (2) a fused-halide mixture, or (3) slags, such as oxides, silicates, alkalis, etc.
Bareis (5) has shown that fused-chloride mixtures will remove rare-earth fission products from dilute solutions of uranium in bismuth.
Brewer (3) has suggested that separations of rare-earths, plutonium, and uranium might be made by selective oxidation of their liquid alloys.
2. precipitation by adding a chemical which reacts with the impurity to form a stable solid compound.
The uranium separates as solid $\mathsf{UBi}_2$ and near $285^{\circ}$ C. only $0.038\%$ U is left dissolved in the liquid bismuth.

FUSED SALT-LIQUID METAL PROCESSES. - The experimental studies of Bareis (5) and the theoretical considerations of Chapter III have dealt primarily with the use of fused salts to treat liquid-metal fuels.
VOLATILE FLUORIDE PROCESSES. - Processes based on UF $_6$ volatilization and involving ClF $_3$ or BrF $_3$ as fluorinating agents are under investigation at several AEC installations (7, 8, 9, 10).

Apparently it is possible to decontaminate uranium by a volatile fluoride process (7), so such a step may be worthy of particular consideration for reactor systems in which UF $_6$ must be prepared for isotopic processing in a gaseous-diffusion plant.
For example, Calkins (11) believed that the UCl₃-UCl₄ system might have a simple eutectic with extensive solid solution formation on the UCl₄ rich side, like the UBr₃-UBr₄ system.
Kraus (12), however, believed that UCl₃ and UCl₄ are only slightly miscible as liquids (See Chapter II, 1.2).
Brewer (3) has suggested several specific processes of this kind.

section: 4. TENTATIVE FLOWSHEETS FOR PROCESSING Bi-U-Pu FUELS
text: Figure IV-4 gives a flowsheet involving three separate steps of fused-salt treatment of the liquid-metal fuel.

section: 4. TENTATIVE FLOWSHEETS FOR PROCESSING Bi-U-Pu FUELS
text: As compared to the flowsheet in Figure IV-3, this flowsheet should permit use of a simpler and smaller wet plant with a less serious shielding problem.

section: 4. TENTATIVE FLOWSHEETS FOR PROCESSING Bi-U-Pu FUELS
text: An alternative flowsheet to meet this difficulty is obtained by substituting a selective-oxidation step for the fused-salt Pu-separation step in Figure IV-4.

section: 4. TENTATIVE FLOWSHEETS FOR PROCESSING Bi-U-Pu FUELS
text: This alternative is based on the expectation that Pu would be oxidized ahead of U (3), to give a plutonium oxide concentrate for feed to the wet plant.

section: 4. TENTATIVE FLOWSHEETS FOR PROCESSING Bi-U-Pu FUELS
text: It would not be surprising if a process such as that shown in Figure IV-4 would allow some fission products to build up in the liquid-metal fuel.

A few fused-chloride mixtures containing UCl₃ and UCl₄ have been studied (8), and several fused-fluoride mixtures containing UF₄ have been studied at Oak Ridge in connection with the ARE project.

Studies of the equilibrium between a single salt and the metal corresponding to its cation, such as those of Eastman, Cubicciotti, and Thurmond (2) should be extended, and an attempt should be made to determine the nature of the species present in the salt phase.

7.2 EXTRACTION WITH FUSED SALTS. - On the basis of present knowledge, the treatment of liquid-metal fuels with fused salts promises to be a very useful process for separation of rare-earth fission-products, U, and Pu from liquid metals.

7.3 GAS-METAL SEPARATIONS. - Some of the fission products may be permanent gases and others may be sufficiently volatile to be separated from the liquid fuel by boiling, vacuum distillation, or flushing with inert gas.
7.4 SELECTIVE OXIDATION. - Many of the procedures of fire refining of common metals are based on selective oxidation.
Brewer suggests that Pu might be separated from liquid U in this way (10), and a similar separation should be feasible if the Pu and U are initially present in liquid Bi.

7.5 PRECIPITATION OF INTERMETALLIC COMPOUNDS. - Work at Ames some years ago indicated that Pu could be precipitated from liquid U in the form of intermetallic compounds with tin and possibly other metals.

7.7 FRACTIONAL CRYSTALLIZATION. - Constitution diagrams and equilibrium data show what separations are possible by cooling and crystallization of liquid alloys, but considerable further research on techniques of phase separation and continuous operation is needed to develop a complete separation process.
7.3 ELECTROLYSIS, LIQUID-METAL ELECTRODES, AND FUSED-SALT ELECTROLYTES.

7.9 SELECTIVE CHLORINATION. - Another process similar in principle to the fused-salt process would involve passing chlorine gas through the liquid metal to form a salt phase of chlorides of metals derived from the liquid fuel.
